themeBlurb: Paper 1 Writing: Situational (14m) + Continuous (36m). 50 marks total. Powered by Composition_Writing_Guide, ShowDontTell_KB, Register_KB, and ModelEssays_Annotated_KB.

composition.title: Continuous Writing — Narrative Craft
composition.blurb: 36-mark narrative composition. Story Mountain, F.A.S.T. characters, R.E.F. endings, Weather Contrast, Flashback techniques.
keyQuestions: Which technique fits this topic — Weather Contrast or Flashback?
Have I shown the emotion through Face / Action / Speech / Thought, not just told it?
Does my climax slow down to land the moment?
Does my ending circle back to the opening image?

glossary.definition: The five-stage arc: Introduction, Rising Action, Climax, Falling Action, Conclusion.
glossary.template: Each stage has its own pacing and emotional purpose.

glossary.definition: Show emotion through Face, Actions, Speech, Thoughts — not by naming it flatly.
glossary.template: Don't write 'she was scared'; show her clenched jaw, her trembling fingers, her racing thoughts.

glossary.definition: The reflective ending: Reflection (what was learned), Emotions (how the character feels now), Future-action (what they will do differently).
glossary.template: Image-anchored, not 'I learnt a valuable lesson'.

glossary.definition: Open and close with weather that mirrors or ironically contrasts the character's inner state. Three mentor essays use this technique.
glossary.template: Ash-grey sky → cleared blue sky as the character finds resolve.

glossary.definition: Open in the present (e.g., hospital, exam results), flash back to the incident, return to the present to reflect.
glossary.template: Signal with: 'Let me take you back to…'

glossary.definition: Hand the camera to another character mid-scene. Used in Mentor Essay 2 when the narrator is unconscious — friends become the eyes that show heroism.
glossary.template: 'What happened next, I did not witness. I learned it later, from the tearful accounts of my friends…'

glossary.definition: Plant a character trait early ('quiet bookish Priya'), then have that character do the opposite at climax. Maximum dramatic lift.
glossary.template: Mentor Essay 2: Priya 'once fainted at a paper cut' → applies pressure to a wound for twelve straight minutes.

composition.learnHTML:
Why this topic matters

Continuous Writing is 36 marks — 18% of the entire PSLE. The gap between AL2 and AL1 here is not about knowing more story rules; it is about showing emotion through body, action, speech, and thought rather than naming it flatly. Read the three Band-5 mentor essays in ModelEssays_Annotated_KB.md once before you draft anything new — that is the calibration target.

The Story Mountain (canonical 5-stage arc)

  1. Introduction: Establish the who, where, what — with a specific sensory anchor. No 'It was a bright sunny day.'
  2. Rising Action: Events leading to the problem; growing tension; character reactions.
  3. Climax: The high-tension turning point. Slow this down. Layer body reactions + inner monologue + dialogue.
  4. Falling Action: Tension eases; problem resolves; consequences play out.
  5. Conclusion (R.E.F.): Reflection + Emotions + Future-action. Image-anchored, not generic moral.

F.A.S.T. — the show-don't-tell lens

When you have written a 'telling' sentence ("she was nervous"), ask the four F.A.S.T. questions:

  • Face — what does the face do? (eyes widened, lips trembled, jaw clenched)
  • Actions — what does the body do? (hands shook, feet rooted to the spot, breath quickened)
  • Speech — what is said or whispered? ("I…I can't," she stammered)
  • Thoughts — what runs through the mind? (every story Mum had ever told me about strangers came flooding back)

Any one of the four lifts a flat line. Two is AL1.

The AL2 → AL1 upgrade table

AL2 habitAL1 upgrade
Generic opening ("One sunny day…")Specific, sensory, or provocative opening
Emotions stated ("He was sad")Emotions shown through body language and action
One-dimensional charactersCharacters with internal conflict and contradictions
Plot-focused storyTheme-focused story (events serve a deeper meaning)
Rushed or generic endingReflective, specific, image-anchored ending
Consistent sentence lengthDeliberate variation (short for action; long for atmosphere)
Memorised phrasesOriginal, precisely chosen expressions

The Climax — where AL1 is won or lost

Most AL2 climaxes rush past the moment in a single sentence ("I was so happy when I saw her"). AL1 climaxes linger. Layer:

  • a body reaction (heart, breath, knees)
  • an inner thought ("It is over," whispered a voice…)
  • a sensory detail from the environment (light, sound, smell)
  • a piece of dialogue, if natural
  • a pause: "Another pause, longer this time, stretched like a wire pulled taut." (Mentor Essay 1)

Three to four short paragraphs at the climax beat one long one every time.

Power vocabulary banks (memorise the top 5 in each)

Said: muttered, snapped, hissed, blurted, stammered, retorted, whispered, exclaimed.

Walked / moved (slow): trudged, staggered, shuffled, clambered, reeled.

Walked / moved (fast): stormed, marched, bolted, sprinted, lunged, darted, hurtled.

Walked / moved (uncoordinated): scrambled, fumbled, tumbled, skidded.

Fear precise: apprehensive, gripped by dread, vertiginous, unnerved, hollow with dread.

Happiness precise: elated, exhilarated, jubilant, euphoric, luminous.

Sadness precise: desolate, crestfallen, bereft, hollow, leaden, wistful.

Anger precise: seething, incensed, indignant, livid, cold-furious.

R.E.F. — the reflective ending

The R.E.F. ending refuses the cliché. Don't write 'I learnt a valuable lesson'. Instead:

  • Reflection: What did this moment mean? Define it as an image, not a sentence-shaped moral.
  • Emotions: How does the character feel now — settled, lighter, changed?
  • Future-action: What will they do differently? (Often left implicit — shown through a final image.)

Mentor Essay 2's closing line — 'Sometimes, bravery is a quiet, bookish girl pressing a cardigan against a wound and refusing to let go — terrified to her core, and doing it anyway, because someone needed her to.' — defines bravery through a portrait, not a sentence-shaped moral.

Three Band-5 mentor essays in knowledge-base/ModelEssays_Annotated_KB.md illustrate every technique. Read each once for the story; once for the craft.
composition.summaryHTML: Three Band-5 mentor essays in ModelEssays_Annotated_KB.md illustrate every technique. The Show-Don't-Tell library (ShowDontTell_KB.md) is the calibration target. Drill the high-frequency idioms in Idioms_Expanded_KB.md so you can deploy 1-2 per piece naturally.
pslePrompts: Open with a specific sensory anchor, not 'It was a bright sunny day'.
One sustained metaphor or motif per essay — set up early, return at climax, land in conclusion.
Use 1–2 idioms per piece, not more. Each must serve the moment.
End with a specific image, not a generic moral.
Numbers under ten: spell them out (first, fifth, ten — not 1st, 5th, 10).

commonTraps.wrong: "It was a bright sunny day…" or "One day, I woke up early."
commonTraps.fix: Open with a specific sensory anchor: a smell, a sound, a colour the reader can see.
commonTraps.why: Generic openings signal AL2 in the first three sentences. Mentor Essay 1 opens with the mynah bird and the smell of cut grass.

commonTraps.wrong: Climax in one rushed sentence: "I was so scared when the dog jumped at me."
commonTraps.fix: Slow the climax into 3–4 short paragraphs. Layer body, thought, environment, dialogue.
commonTraps.why: The climax is where AL1 is decided. Lingering makes the moment matter; rushing makes it feel cheap.

commonTraps.wrong: "I learnt a valuable lesson that day." / "That was the best day of my life."
commonTraps.fix: End with a specific image and an implicit future-action. R.E.F., not a moral sentence.
commonTraps.why: These are banned phrases. They tell, not show, and signal a memorised template.

commonTraps.wrong: Character is "looking forward to a day" but suddenly "taps their foot impatiently" with no trigger.
commonTraps.fix: Every emotion shift needs a cause. Plant the trigger in the previous paragraph.
commonTraps.why: Inconsistent characterisation is one of the four logic errors examiners flag.

commonTraps.wrong: Stacked emotion words: "I was very, very, very sad."
commonTraps.fix: Replace with one precise verb or image: "hollow", "a knot tightened in my chest".
commonTraps.why: Intensifiers (very, really, so) are AL2 markers. Precise vocabulary is AL1.

masteryCheckpoints: Your climax is detailed in 3–4 short paragraphs, not one rushed sentence.
Every named emotion is shown through at least one F.A.S.T. dimension (Face, Action, Speech, or Thought).
Your opening is a specific sensory image — not "It was a sunny day".
Your ending is an image, not a moral sentence.
You can point to one sustained metaphor or motif that appears at opening, climax, and closing.
You vary sentence length deliberately — short for action and shock, long for atmosphere.

drillItems.prompt: Best opening hook for a story about losing your dog at the park:
options: It was a sunny day at the park.
Buddy's leash slid through my fingers like wet rope.
I will tell you about when I lost my dog.
Once upon a time my dog ran away.
drillItems.explanation: A sensory action hook drops the reader inside the moment. Telling openings ('It was a sunny day') waste your first 10 marks of impression.

drillItems.prompt: Which sentence SHOWS fear, not just tells it? (F.A.S.T.)
options: I was very scared.
I felt scared and afraid.
My fingers froze around the door handle; my heartbeat thudded in my ears.
Fear filled my entire body completely.
drillItems.explanation: F.A.S.T. = Face/Actions/Speech/Thoughts. Physical signals beat adjectives. 'Froze' and 'thudded' are bodily detail.

drillItems.prompt: Pick the most precise verb to replace 'walked quickly and worriedly' in: 'I ___ down the corridor.'
options: hurried
scurried
marched
ambled
drillItems.explanation: 'Scurried' carries both speed AND anxiety. 'Hurried' is fine; 'scurried' is sharper. 'Marched' is confident; 'ambled' is slow.

drillItems.prompt: Weather Contrast technique — which line uses it best?
options: The sun was shining and I was happy.
It was raining and I felt sad.
The sun blazed cheerfully on what would become the worst afternoon of my life.
I was sad. The weather was sad too.
drillItems.explanation: Weather Contrast = mood opposes weather. 'Blazed cheerfully' against 'worst afternoon' creates dramatic irony. Matching mood and weather is the cliché.

drillItems.prompt: Which is a banned PSLE composition phrase?
options: My heart pounded against my ribs.
I learnt a valuable lesson that day.
The corridor smelled of old paint.
I never wanted to feel that way again.
drillItems.explanation: Examiners flag 'I learnt a valuable lesson' as a cliché ending. Replace with R.E.F. — Reflection, Emotion, Future-action — in your own words.

drillItems.prompt: R.E.F. ending — which line FAILS the test?
options: I would never look at my brother the same way again.
From that day on, I made sure my homework was done before football.
That was the end of my story.
Even now, the smell of fresh paint takes me back.
drillItems.explanation: 'That was the end of my story' is filler, not reflection. R.E.F. needs a change in the narrator, not a stage announcement.

drillItems.prompt: Most natural dialogue tag for a tense whisper:
options: he said loudly
he shouted angrily
he hissed
he ejaculated
drillItems.explanation: 'Hissed' carries volume (quiet) and emotion (tense) in one verb. 'Said loudly' contradicts a whisper. 'Ejaculated' has another modern meaning — avoid.

drillItems.prompt: Best place to put the CLIMAX in a 200-word PSLE composition:
options: Paragraph 1 — open with the climax to grab attention.
Paragraph 2 — get to it quickly.
Paragraph 3 of 4 — after build-up, before resolution.
Paragraph 4 — save the best for last.
drillItems.explanation: Standard PSLE arc: Opening → Build-up → Climax → Resolution+Reflection. A premature climax leaves nothing for paragraph 3.

drillItems.prompt: Which simile is FRESH (not a cliché)?
options: as quiet as a mouse
as quick as lightning
his face turned as still as glass left out in the cold
as busy as a bee
drillItems.explanation: The first three are tired similes examiners groan at. 'Still as glass left out in the cold' is specific and observed.

drillItems.prompt: How long should a flashback be in a 200-word composition?
options: One full paragraph, fully developed.
Two or three sentences — a quick cut, then back to now.
Half the composition — flashback is the story.
Avoid flashbacks at PSLE level.
drillItems.explanation: At 200 words, a flashback longer than three sentences eats your build-up budget. Quick cut: italic-feeling sentence, then 'I shook the memory away.'

drillItems.prompt: Strongest variant of: 'I was angry at my sister.'
options: I was very angry at my sister.
My sister made me angry.
A hot pulse rose up my throat; my fists clenched at my sides.
I felt anger towards my sister.
drillItems.explanation: F.A.S.T. — bodily signals (hot pulse, clenched fists) SHOW anger. The other three TELL it.

drillItems.prompt: Best transition between Build-up and Climax:
options: Then suddenly,
And then,
Without warning, the floor seemed to drop away beneath me.
Next,
drillItems.explanation: A full sentence transition with sensory detail beats stock conjunctions. 'Suddenly' is fine once per story, not as the climax cue.

questions.question: Which of these opening sentences best follows AL1 'show, don't tell' principles?
options: I was very scared as I walked into the dark room.
My knees trembled and I held my breath as the door creaked open behind me.
It was a very scary moment that I will never forget.
I felt afraid because the room was dark and quiet.
questions.explanation: Option B shows fear through Face (held breath) and Actions (trembling knees) without using the word 'scared'. This is the F.A.S.T. lens in action — body does the work, not narration.

questions.question: Which ending best demonstrates the R.E.F. (Reflection, Emotions, Future-action) framework?
options: I learnt a valuable lesson that day.
I never forgot that day.
As I rinsed the chlorine from my hair, the memory of his outstretched hand stayed with me. I would never look away again.
That was the best day of my life and I will always remember it.
questions.explanation: Option C anchors the reflection in a specific image (rinsing chlorine), conveys the emotion implicitly, and signals future-action ('never look away again'). Options A, B, D are banned generic endings.

questions.question: In the mentor essay 'A Day I Will Never Forget', the Weather Contrast technique works because:
options: The narrator is good at describing weather.
The bright sunshine outside ironically contrasts with the narrator's inner guilt — the world keeps shining while he suffers.
Sunshine is a happy symbol in stories.
The weather changes during the story.
questions.explanation: The Weather Contrast technique uses external weather to mirror or ironically contrast the character's inner emotional state. The mentor essay opens bright/inner-storm and closes bright/inner-truth — same external, transformed internal.

questions.question: Mentor Essay 2 ('The Bravest Thing') uses which structural technique?
options: Weather Contrast.
Flashback — opens in the present (hospital), flashes back to the cycling accident, returns to the present to reflect.
A single linear narrative with no time shifts.
Multiple narrators speaking in turn.
questions.explanation: Mentor Essay 2 opens in the hospital ('The first thing I noticed was the smell — antiseptic, sharp and clinical'), signals the flashback with 'Let me take you back to the afternoon that put me in that hospital bed', and returns to the present in the falling action. This contrast between then-and-now creates emotional resonance.

questions.question: The 'AL2 → AL1 upgrade table' suggests that 'consistent sentence length' should become:
options: Always short sentences for clarity.
Always long sentences for sophistication.
Deliberate variation — short for action and shock, longer for atmosphere and suspense.
Random length, whatever feels natural.
questions.explanation: AL1 rewards deliberate sentence rhythm. Mentor Essay 1 demonstrates this: 'The hall fell silent. I had forgotten my lines.' (short, shock) followed by a long descriptive sentence builds suspense. The variation itself signals craft.

situational.title: Situational Writing — 14 Marks
situational.blurb: Functional writing across seven genres. Six content points (one is the underlined critical-thinking point not in the stimulus).
keyQuestions: What is the Purpose, Audience, Context, and Writer-persona (PACW)?
Have I addressed all six content points, including the underlined one?
Does my register match the genre (formal letter vs informal email)?
Does the salutation match the sign-off?

glossary.definition: Purpose, Audience, Context, Writer-persona — the four dimensions to lock in before writing.
glossary.template: Identify all four before drafting a single sentence.

glossary.definition: One of the six content points is underlined and NOT in the stimulus. You must invent a specific, realistic detail.
glossary.template: Don't write 'it is good'; write 'it reduced our weekly waste by 32 kg'.

glossary.definition: The level of formality — formal, neutral, or informal. Set by Purpose, Audience, and Context together.
glossary.template: Formal letter to principal: 'I am writing to express...'; informal email to friend: 'Hi! Guess what...'.

glossary.definition: The opening and closing must agree on formality. Mismatching costs format marks.
glossary.template: Dear Sir/Madam ↔ Yours faithfully. Dear Mr Tan ↔ Yours sincerely. Hi John ↔ Take care.

glossary.definition: Formal vs informal vocabulary alternatives that signal register precisely.
glossary.template: ask→enquire, get→obtain, tell→inform, start→commence, try→attempt, find out→discover.

situational.learnHTML:
Situational Writing — 14 marks, ~25 minutes

You are given a SITUATION and asked to write a functional piece. You must:

  1. Use the correct FORMAT for the text type
  2. ADDRESS all six content points (one is underlined — that information is not in the stimulus; you must generate it)
  3. Use the correct REGISTER (formal or informal tone)
Missing one bullet point typically costs 2–3 marks. Missing the underlined critical-thinking point is the most common loss because it tests independent reasoning.

The seven genres (2025+)

GenreDefault registerKey conventions
Formal letterFormalAddress top-right; date; Dear Sir/Madam ↔ Yours faithfully; Dear Mr Tan ↔ Yours sincerely; no contractions.
Informal email / letterInformalHi [first name] ↔ Take care; contractions OK; conversational fillers ("Guess what?").
ReportFormal-neutralTitle; sections; numbered findings + recommendations; avoid "I" — use "the writer" / "this report".
SpeechMid-formalAddress audience directly ("Good morning everyone…"); rhetorical questions allowed; vary sentence length.
Article (new 2025)Mid-formalCatchy headline + byline required; opening hook; quotation marks for cited speech; closes with reflection or call to action.
ReviewInformal–mid-formalFirst person allowed; balanced (good points + bad); recommendation at the end.
NoticeFormal-neutralTitle in CAPS; date; bullet points; key info first; signed with role; no "I" — notices are impersonal.

The 10-minute formula

  1. 1 min — Analyse: Identify PACW and the underlined point.
  2. 1 min — Number: Mark all six content points on the stimulus.
  3. 1 min — Decide: Finalise tone and format conventions.
  4. 7 min — Draft: Write, ticking off each numbered point.

The critical-thinking point — worked example

A task asks you to write to the principal sharing about a recycling programme. Five points are in the stimulus poster. The sixth is underlined: 'the impact of the programme on the school.' The poster does NOT tell you the impact. You must invent a specific, realistic detail:

  • Weak: 'It is good for the environment.'
  • Strong: 'We reduced our weekly waste by 32 kg.'
  • Strong: 'Two classes have started a compost garden using the food scraps.'

Formal openers — eight purpose-matched

  • Complaint: "I am writing to express my disappointment with…"
  • Request: "I am writing to request your permission for…"
  • Enquiry: "I am writing to enquire about…"
  • Thanks: "I am writing to express our sincere gratitude for…"
  • Suggestion: "I am writing to propose / to suggest that…"
  • Apology: "I am writing to apologise for…"
  • Information: "I am writing to inform you that…"
  • Invitation: "I am writing to extend a cordial invitation to…"

Swap pairs — formal vocabulary upgrades

Replace casual verbs with precise alternatives. The Cloze and Editing components reward these too.

  • ask → enquire / request
  • help → assist / support
  • get → obtain / receive
  • tell → inform / notify
  • show → demonstrate / indicate
  • start → commence / initiate
  • end → conclude / terminate
  • try → attempt / endeavour
  • think → consider / believe
  • want → wish / require
  • need → require
  • give → provide / supply
  • find out → discover / ascertain
  • put off → postpone / defer
  • set up → establish / arrange
  • look into → investigate / examine

Register self-check (30-second drill before submitting)

  1. Salutation matches sign-off? Dear Mr Tan ↔ Yours sincerely ✓. Mismatch costs marks.
  2. No contractions in formal pieces? Search for 't and 's.
  3. No slang or Singlish? Search for lah, lor, cool, awesome, guys.
  4. Verbs upgraded? Are get / ask / tell replaced with obtain / enquire / inform in a formal piece?
  5. Tone consistent? No sudden jokes in a complaint; no sudden coldness in a thank-you.
  6. Closer matches purpose? Action-seeking → "I look forward to your prompt reply"; closed → "Thank you for your time".
situational.summaryHTML: Situational Writing is 14 marks. Six content points to address (one underlined critical-thinking). Marking: Task Fulfilment 6 + Language & Organisation 8. The seven genres are detailed in Composition_Writing_Guide.md, with a full register matrix in Register_KB.md.
pslePrompts: Number all six content points on the stimulus before writing.
The underlined point is the most common one missed. Invent a specific, realistic detail.
Match salutation and sign-off: Dear Sir/Madam → Yours faithfully; Dear Mr Tan → Yours sincerely; Hi John → Take care.
No contractions in formal writing (do not, cannot, will not).
Article (new 2025): MUST have a catchy headline and a byline at the top.

commonTraps.wrong: "Dear Mr Tan… Yours faithfully" or "Dear Sir/Madam… Yours sincerely".
commonTraps.fix: Dear Sir/Madam ↔ Yours FAITHFULLY (name unknown). Dear Mr Tan ↔ Yours SINCERELY (name known).
commonTraps.why: Mismatching salutation and sign-off is one of the most common 1-mark losses in Situational Writing.

commonTraps.wrong: Article without a headline or byline.
commonTraps.fix: Always include a catchy headline AND a byline (e.g., "By Aisha Lim, P6 Sunrise Primary") above the body.
commonTraps.why: Article was added as a genre in 2025. The headline+byline format is now mandatory.

commonTraps.wrong: Report written like a letter — "I think" / "I feel".
commonTraps.fix: Reports are impersonal: "This report finds that…" or "The writer recommends…". Use sections + numbered points.
commonTraps.why: Reports test your ability to organise findings objectively. First-person opinion signals genre confusion.

commonTraps.wrong: Notice with "I" in it ("I am writing to tell you…").
commonTraps.fix: Notices are impersonal announcements. Skip the writer altogether. Title in CAPS, key info first, sign with role.
commonTraps.why: Notices serve the community, not the writer. "I" makes it sound like an email.

commonTraps.wrong: Formal letter with contractions ("I'd like to express…" / "don't" / "can't").
commonTraps.fix: Spell out: "I would like to express…", "do not", "cannot".
commonTraps.why: Contractions are informal markers. They cost Language & Organisation marks even when the rest is good.

commonTraps.wrong: Critical-thinking point answered vaguely: "It is good for the school."
commonTraps.fix: Invent a specific, realistic detail: "We reduced our weekly waste by 32 kg" or "Two classes started a compost garden".
commonTraps.why: Vague claims score zero on the critical-thinking point because they could apply to anything. Specificity earns the mark.

masteryCheckpoints: You identify all six content points and tick them off as you write (especially the underlined one).
The underlined critical-thinking point has a specific, realistic detail invented — not a vague claim.
Salutation and sign-off match perfectly (Faithfully ↔ no name; Sincerely ↔ named).
Register is consistent throughout — no contractions, no slang in formal pieces.
You can name the genre's format conventions in 5 seconds (article = headline + byline; notice = caps + bullet points; report = numbered findings).
You used at least 3 "swap pair" upgrades (obtain, enquire, inform, commence, etc.) in a formal piece.

drillItems.prompt: Opening salutation for an email to your school principal:
options: Hi Mrs Lim,
Dear Mrs Lim,
Yo Mrs Lim,
Hello there,
drillItems.explanation: Formal authority figure → 'Dear'. Reserve 'Hi' for peers.

drillItems.prompt: Sign-off for a letter to a classmate apologising for a mistake:
options: Yours sincerely,
Yours faithfully,
Your friend,
Regards,
drillItems.explanation: Peer + warmth → 'Your friend' or 'Take care'. 'Yours sincerely' is for known-name formal letters.

drillItems.prompt: Which point is MISSING from this email? 'Dear Mr Lee, I am writing to thank you for your coaching. The drill on first-touch helped me a lot. Yours sincerely, Alex.'
options: A subject line.
An apology for missing one training session.
A question about next term's trial.
Both B and C — and you would not know which is missing without the question stem.
drillItems.explanation: Situational marks come from checking off EVERY bullet in the question. Read the bullet list twice before drafting.

drillItems.prompt: Best opening sentence for a NOTE to a classmate (60–80 words):
options: I am writing to inform you that I broke your bottle.
Hi Rachel, sorry about your water bottle — I knocked it off the desk by accident.
Dear Rachel, with great respect I write to apologise for the unfortunate incident.
Bottle broken. Sorry.
drillItems.explanation: Note + peer → friendly + direct. Option A is too formal; C is over-grand; D too brief and rude.

drillItems.prompt: Tone for a formal letter to the principal requesting a new club:
options: Casual and chatty
Polite, formal, persuasive
Demanding and urgent
Apologetic and uncertain
drillItems.explanation: Authority figure + request = polite, formal, persuasive (reasons + benefits). Demanding alienates; casual disrespects the format.

drillItems.prompt: Required for a Letter but NOT for an Email or Note:
options: A salutation
A sign-off
A subject / Re: line
Sender's address
drillItems.explanation: Formal letters include sender's address top-right (or top-left in modern style). Emails put the subject in a separate line; notes need neither address nor subject.

drillItems.prompt: Best subject line for an email asking your form teacher for a make-up assessment:
options: Hi
Important
Request to take a make-up Maths CA on 18 May
PLEASE READ
drillItems.explanation: Specific + actionable. The reader knows what you want before opening.

drillItems.prompt: Which line breaks the register of a formal letter?
options: I would be most grateful if you could consider this proposal.
I look forward to your reply.
Lol if you say yes that would be lit.
Thank you for taking the time to read this letter.
drillItems.explanation: 'Lol' and 'lit' are casual; they crash the register. The graders score formal tone; don't sabotage it.

drillItems.prompt: Polite way to disagree with a teacher in an email:
options: You are wrong, sir.
I respectfully think there may be a different way to look at this.
No, I don't think so.
That's a stupid idea.
drillItems.explanation: 'Respectfully' + 'may be' hedges. PSLE awards polite hedging in formal exchanges with authority figures.

drillItems.prompt: How many of the listed bullet points must your situational reply cover?
options: All of them.
About half — pick the most important.
At least one.
It depends on the word limit.
drillItems.explanation: All required points must be covered for full Task Fulfilment marks. Missing one usually drops you a band.

drillItems.prompt: Most appropriate closing for a letter to your principal:
options: See ya soon!
Yours sincerely,
Yours faithfully,
Love,
drillItems.explanation: When you know the recipient's name (Mrs Lim) → 'Yours sincerely'. 'Yours faithfully' is for 'Dear Sir/Madam'.

drillItems.prompt: Which approach gets you marks fastest in a 60–80 word situational reply?
options: Start by introducing yourself in detail.
Mention each required bullet point in one sentence each, then close.
Save the bullets for the second paragraph.
Use long, flowery sentences.
drillItems.explanation: 60–80 words gives you ~5–7 sentences. One sentence per required point + salutation + sign-off = perfect cover. Length is too tight for filler.

questions.question: Which salutation-and-sign-off pair is correct for a formal letter to a school principal whose name is Mr Tan?
options: Dear Sir/Madam ... Yours sincerely
Dear Mr Tan ... Yours faithfully
Dear Mr Tan ... Yours sincerely
Hi Mr Tan ... Take care
questions.explanation: When the recipient's name IS known: 'Dear Mr Tan ... Yours sincerely'. When the name is NOT known: 'Dear Sir/Madam ... Yours faithfully'. Option D is informal and inappropriate for a principal.

questions.question: The underlined content point in a Situational Writing task asks you to mention 'the impact of the recycling programme'. The stimulus poster does NOT contain this information. The best response is:
options: Skip this point; you cannot answer what is not in the stimulus.
Write 'the impact is very good for the environment'.
Invent a specific, realistic detail such as 'we reduced our weekly waste by 32 kg'.
Copy a different point from the stimulus instead.
questions.explanation: The underlined point is the 'critical-thinking' content point — it is NOT in the stimulus by design. You must generate a specific, realistic, plausible detail. Vague claims like 'very good' score zero.

questions.question: A formal email to the council should use which opener?
options: Hey there!
What's up? I wanted to say...
I am writing to express my concern about the rising number of accidents at the pedestrian crossing.
OMG you have to fix the crossing!
questions.explanation: Formal openers begin with 'I am writing to express / enquire / request / inform / propose...'. Options A, B, D are informal markers that lose register marks immediately.

questions.question: An Article (new 2025 genre) requires which of these at the top?
options: Just a title in capitals.
An address and a date.
A catchy headline AND a byline (writer's name + role).
A salutation like 'Dear Reader'.
questions.explanation: The Article genre, new in 2025, requires both a catchy headline and a byline (e.g., 'By Aisha Lim, P6 Sunrise Primary'). It's neither a letter nor a report, so don't use salutations or address blocks.

questions.question: Which is a register-trap that loses formal-letter marks?
options: Using long sentences.
Using contractions like "I'd", "don't", "can't".
Using polite phrases like "Could you kindly…".
Numbering your points clearly.
questions.explanation: Contractions are informal markers and cost Language & Organisation marks in formal letters. Always spell out: "I would", "do not", "cannot". Long sentences and polite phrases are fine.
